A note on sending email with Nodemailer. Covers two approaches with Gmail — OAuth2, and username/password (App Password).
I'll provide a base environment for you:
This is a simple Express scaffold with
installed — everything else is Express generator boilerplate.
If you'd rather not use the template, build your own Express project and install:
language-bash shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
I wrote an earlier post on
Full-stack Warrior's Path Node.js - OAuth 2.0 & nodemailer & Gmail (article in Mandarin)
, but that's pretty outdated now, so I'm rewriting.
What's Nodemailer? It's a Node.js package for sending email. Sites really need this — verification codes, notification emails, subscription emails — Email functionality comes up all the time.
The official docs include a fairly complete example:
language-js shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
// async..await is not allowed in global scope, must use a wrapper
// send mail with defined transport object
Our goal is to send Email through Gmail with OAuth2, so let's see how to do that.
First, Gmail has some limits worth knowing:
Other things — spam, unsolicited ads, virus emails — are also not allowed. Google might lock your account, so be careful.

Older Nodemailer tutorials used username/password. Google has since restricted that since it's less secure, pushing OAuth2 instead. It's more complex because you need to handle authorization, so I'll start with the harder approach.
and create a new project. You could reuse an old one but a new one keeps things cleaner.
Pick whatever name. I called mine "nodemailer-example".
Once created, find it in the notification or in the project picker.
Click the hamburger menu on the left, then "APIs & Services Enabled".
Click "ENABLE APIS AND SERVICES" at the top.
Before creating credentials, if it's your first time, you'll be prompted to configure the OAuth consent screen. Click "Configure consent screen".
In "Edit app registration", fill in the required fields (others are optional):
If unsure, just follow the image:

"Test users" page — if your app is in "Testing" state, you need to add test users who can use the app. Click "Add Users" and add your own Google account. For production, you don't need this. I'll skip and click "Save and continue".
Once you reach "Summary", OAuth consent screen setup is done.
Back to "Credentials", click "Create Credentials", then "OAuth client ID".
Choose "Web application" for application type, then:
In an earlier post, I mentioned using
for this. We're using a different approach now.
Click "Create" — you'll get a Client ID and Client Secret. Don't leak them. If they're misused, Google may lock your account. (You can also download a JSON file with both values.)
That wraps up the Gmail OAuth setup. Now to the code.
I provided the base environment earlier, so we can dive right in.
are installed, or you'll hit errors.
In the project root, create a
language-env shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast

CLIENT_ID=your Client ID
CLIENT_SECRET=your Client Secret
REDIRECT_URI=http://localhost:3000/auth/google/callback
These come from the credential we just created. Or download the JSON file — it has everything:
folder in the root, with a file
to get the OAuth2 URL — you'll be redirected to Google's login page.
Pick an account. You'll get a warning screen — don't worry, it's your own app. Click "Advanced" then "Go to nodemailer-example (unsafe)".
so we can use them later.
Error authenticating with Google: TypeError: Cannot set properties of undefined (setting 'tokens')
This is because Express generator doesn't enable
This fixes the "TypeError: Cannot set properties of undefined" error.
One subtle gotcha — the docs example uses
. In local dev, your session won't persist with that — it gets cleared on every page navigation. I'm using
so session works in local dev.
Nodemailer setup is done. You can send emails from the page now.

The OAuth approach has a limitation — if today's logged-in user isn't A but B, you can't send because the Token and Refresh Token belong to A. So I want to show another way — hardcode the username and password to bypass this.
Many people who've tried username/password with Gmail have hit:

Error: Invalid login: 535-5.7.8 Username and Password not accepted. Learn more at 535 5.7.8  https://support.google.com/mail/?p=BadCredentials k17-20020aa78211000000b00682562bf477sm3695625pfi.82 - gsmtp
Because Google restricted username/password sending. Used to be you'd just turn on "Less secure app access" in Google settings — but that's been removed.
Next, we configure Google — but it's not as complex as before.
Go to Google Account settings, click "Security".
If you don't have 2-Step Verification enabled, do that first or you can't continue.
Tested with a new account — Google now requires 2-Step Verification.
With 2-Step Verification on, click into it, enter your password, scroll down to find "App passwords".
It'll prompt for a name — name it "nodemailer-example" and click "Generate".
You'll get a 16-character password. Save it carefully — this is what we use:
Google updates this UI often, so my article can fall behind. If you spot a change, let me know.
Below is the updated UI as of October 20, 2023. If you can't find "App passwords", search "App passwords" at the top:
Enter "nodemail" in App Name and click "Create".
You'll get a random-looking password. Save it.
Time to write code. Fill in your sender email and the App Password into .env:

GMAIL_USER=example@gmail.com # don't worry, this is fake
GMAIL_PASS=d81ydvsldqiwdhnc # don't worry, this is fake
GMAIL_USER=example@gmail.com # don't worry, this is fake
GMAIL_PASS=d81ydvsldqiwdhnc # don't worry, this is fake
The generated app password might have spaces — strip them.
You're sending emails now — not through OAuth2 but through username/password — without the limitation I mentioned.
You can trigger sending from a UI button directly. For actual production use, you'll need to adjust further.
Honestly this is a kind of messy, scattered topic. Online resources are all over the place — I tried a bunch of times to get it working. Hopefully this helps.
